entry.title: Master & Commander (Aubrey & Maturin, #1)
entry.summary:
by Patrick O'Brian

✮✮✮✮☆

Good, though it took me a little while to get into it. I was constantly referring to the chart of sails in front-- the technical language is a little out of control, although I got enough of a feel for it that the battles toward the middle-end …
entry.content:
by Patrick O'Brian

✮✮✮✮☆

Good, though it took me a little while to get into it. I was constantly referring to the chart of sails in front-- the technical language is a little out of control, although I got enough of a feel for it that the battles toward the middle-end were actually exciting.

The only real complaint I have is that some of the characters felt more like they were put in because "look how much research I did!" coughgayofficercough than because they were important to the story.

entry.title: The Yiddish Policemen's Union
entry.summary:
by Michael Chabon

✮✮✮✮✮

Rereading, 6/22/2013. (Has it been 6 years? Wow.) There's a moment where Landsman tries to speak American without an accent, and it made me think of my grandfather's new york jewish cadence, and in that moment I remembered that he's gone and so is much …
entry.content:
by Michael Chabon

✮✮✮✮✮

Rereading, 6/22/2013. (Has it been 6 years? Wow.) There's a moment where Landsman tries to speak American without an accent, and it made me think of my grandfather's new york jewish cadence, and in that moment I remembered that he's gone and so is much of his world.

I think I like it even more on reread, though I'm not sure it'll upset Kavalier and Clay as my favorite Chabon book. My yiddish vocabulary has grown, if only a little---I really appreciated all the plays and metaphors on "faygele," dancing lightly around the word itself but never saying it. I desperately want the dead man and Landsman's sister to survive; I have to remind myself again and again that their fates were known from the first pages, and I grieve them all over again.

I don't know how insightful and organized this review is going to be....

Finally finished this one off-- and I want to read it again! Sometimes the writing was a little pretentious (like the lists of objects, which you can tell Chabon likes to do), but it was always a great read. I loved the Yiddish words and invented slang; Sitka came alive for me, in its tired, ramshackle, and thoroughly Jewish way. Often, I was reading more for Sitka than the plot or any of the other characters.

The plot towards the end began to remind me of Neal Stephenson in its conspiracy-filled unlikeliness, but it was still interesting.

entry.title: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows (Harry Potter, #7)
entry.summary:
by J.K. Rowling

✮✮✮✮☆

Okay so technically this is a reread, but I think I get to count one of them here, since I just finished a reread of the entire series. That's a couple thousand pages, no?

You know what? I liked the wandering-around-in-the-woods part when I first read …
entry.content:
by J.K. Rowling

✮✮✮✮☆

Okay so technically this is a reread, but I think I get to count one of them here, since I just finished a reread of the entire series. That's a couple thousand pages, no?

You know what? I liked the wandering-around-in-the-woods part when I first read it (over 2 days, because I was trying to make it last)... and on reread, I still like it. To each hir own.

Even the epilogue is less annoying on reread, although possibly that's because I'm still firm in my decision that it's not actually part of the book.

entry.title: Freakonomics: A Rogue Economist Explores the Hidden Side of Everything
entry.content:
by Steven D. Levitt, Stephen J. Dubner

no star rating

entry.title: A Civil Campaign (Vorkosigan Saga, #12)
entry.content:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

✮✮✮✮☆

This is the Vorkosigan book I reread the least often, probably because I have a fierce embarrassment squick. It's very good though, and thoughtful. Appropriate reading, since I've been to three weddings so far this year, including two just this month!

by Franklin Foer

✮✮✮✮☆

I still think about this book sometimes, 10 years on. Ripe for a reread.
